disease.name: Pediatric Epiglottitis

ageGate.note: Hib vaccine era: incidence has dramatically decreased. Now more common in older children and adults. Unvaccinated children remain at risk.

disease.note: Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) was the dominant cause before routine Hib vaccination. Now Hib is less common but still occurs in unvaccinated children. Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes, and Streptococcus pneumoniae are now relatively more common.

immediateDangerSigns.description: Inability to swallow saliva due to painful swollen epiglottis
Sitting upright, leaning forward, chin thrust out, mouth open — attempts to maximize airway

severeCriteria.description: Any danger sign indicates imminent airway compromise. Do NOT examine throat. Prepare for emergency airway.

historyFeatures.description: Severe painful swallowing — child refuses to swallow

historyFeatures.note: Unvaccinated children are at higher risk

disease.examWarningNote: NEVER examine the throat or attempt to visualize the epiglottis without an airway team present and equipment for emergency intubation ready. Direct visualization can precipitate complete airway obstruction.

riskFactors.note: Hib vaccine has dramatically reduced incidence

laboratory.name: Epiglottic swab for culture
laboratory.indication: obtained by anaesthetist during airway visualization

imaging.name: Lateral neck X-ray
imaging.priority: only if diagnostic uncertainty and child is stable
imaging.contraindication: Do NOT send child to radiology if unstable. A stable child should NEVER be left alone in radiology.
imaging.note: If epiglottitis is strongly suspected clinically, do NOT wait for imaging — proceed to controlled airway management.

investigations.warning: Do NOT attempt to visualize the epiglottis with a tongue depressor. This can cause laryngospasm and complete airway obstruction. Visualization is done by the anaesthetist during controlled intubation in the operating theatre.

management.protocolReference: Kenya Paediatric Guidelines + Pediatric Epiglottitis Emergency Airway Protocol

airwayManagement.priority: AIRWAY IS THE FIRST PRIORITY. Do not leave the child unattended.
steps: Keep child upright and calm — minimize disturbance (allow child to remain in parent's lap in tripod position)
Immediate consultation with anaesthesia and ENT for controlled intubation in operating theatre
DO NOT attempt IV access or blood draws before airway is secured if child is stable — minimal handling
Gas induction (sevoflurane/halothane) with child in sitting position, maintaining spontaneous ventilation
Endotracheal tube 0.5-1 size smaller than age-appropriate due to supraglottic edema
Have rigid bronchoscopy equipment ready for failed airway
Once intubated: sedation + ventilation in ICU

extubationCriteria: Evidence of air leak around ETT
Direct laryngoscopy showing resolution of edema (performed by ENT)

antibioticTherapy.initialEmpiric: IV Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily (or Cefotaxime 50 mg/kg every 8h)
antibioticTherapy.plusMetronidazole: IV Metronidazole 7.5 mg/kg every 8h if anaerobic co-infection suspected (retropharyngeal extension)
antibioticTherapy.note: Adjust based on culture results once available

supportiveCare: ICU admission for all confirmed cases
Continue sedation and ventilation until airway edema subsides
Treat fever with paracetamol 10-15 mg/kg every 6h PRN
Corticosteroids: dexamethasone 0.15-0.3 mg/kg IV may reduce airway edema (limited evidence but commonly used)

pathways.criteria: Acute onset fever, drooling, tripod position, no cough, toxic child
action: Immediate anaesthesia + ENT consultation for controlled intubation
Do not send for imaging if unstable
IV Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once airway secured

treatmentResponseLogic.expectedCourse: After intubation and antibiotics: clinical improvement within 24-48 hours. Extubation usually possible within 48-72 hours.
treatmentResponseLogic.positiveResponse: Reduced fever, reduced airway edema on laryngoscopy, air leak around ETT, hemodynamic stability.

failureToExtubate.action: Repeat laryngoscopy, consider CT neck, continue antibiotics, consider steroids

complications.note: Risk reduced with appropriate size ETT and careful technique

disease.emergencyPriority: TRUE AIRWAY EMERGENCY. Do NOT examine throat. Do NOT agitate child. Immediate anaesthesia/ENT for controlled intubation. No investigations before airway is secured. This is one of the few pediatric conditions where the airway takes priority over all else.
